advantages: Matrices provide a compact, uniform representation for batches of linear relationships, letting you operate on entire datasets with a few array operations.
Entrywise addition and scalar multiplication are simple, parallel-friendly operations that map directly to optimized BLAS and GPU kernels.
Transpose, slicing and reshaping make it easy to reorient data (examples vs features) without copying underlying semantics.
Square matrices can encode invertible linear maps, enabling direct solution of linear systems and closed-form updates in some models.
disadvantages: Shape constraints are strict: incompatible dimensions for addition or multiplication fail at runtime and are a frequent source of bugs.
Dense matrix storage uses O(mn) memory, which becomes prohibitive for very wide or very tall datasets without sparsity or streaming.
Not every modeling idea is linear; forcing nonlinear structure into pure matrix form can obscure the real computation or require approximations.
Ill-conditioned matrices amplify numerical error during inversion or decomposition, leading to unstable training or misleading solutions.
